text.title: SLO Burn Decision Matrix Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the SLO Burn Decision Matrix methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Burn categories MUST be explicitly enumerated: fast (>=14.4x for 1h), medium (>=6x for 6h), slow (>=1x for 3d). No alert outside these categories triggers a matrix action.

rationale.source: Google SRE Workbook — Alerting on SLOs

rule.rationale: Without explicit boundaries, teams treat every alert as 'whatever I feel'. Google SRE 4-window multi-burn-rate gives concrete bounds.

rule.statement: Every (burn_category, service_class) cell MUST have a single named action: halt-canary, freeze-ship, focus-shift, accept. No 'depends'; pick one default.
rule.rationale: Multi-option cells re-introduce the debate the matrix exists to prevent.

rule.statement: Each action carries a named owner role (SRE on-call, release manager, service owner). Owner is paged simultaneously with the burn alert.
rule.rationale: Unowned actions stall in 'who does this?'; named role removes ambiguity.

rule.statement: Each action has explicit revert criteria (burn rate < 1.0 for 6h, error budget regenerated by X%). The revert is automatic once criteria met; no separate decision required.
rule.rationale: Without revert criteria, freezes outlast their cause and become political.

rule.statement: Every matrix-triggered action emits an audit event (timestamp, category, service_class, action, owner, revert_at). Logs stored ≥1 year for trend analysis.
rule.rationale: Audit logs surface miscalibrated matrices (the same action triggered 50x last quarter) and enable post-hoc tuning.

rule.statement: 'accept' as an action requires an explicit cell entry. Missing cells default to 'pause and escalate to engineering leader', NOT 'accept silently'.
rule.rationale: Accept-by-default is the failure mode that makes the matrix worthless.
